For all the damage Encarnacion-Strand does at the plate, scouts don't love his right-handed swing. He has an unconventional load and an uphill bat path that leave him vulnerable to high velocity and quality breaking balls. His proponents believe a pro team could help him improve his stroke and get 25 homers per year out of him if he also mitigates an aggressive approach. There are questions about Encarnacion-Strand's defensive home as well. He has the hands and strong arm to remain at third base, but he's a well below-average runner with limited range. His only alternative would be first base unless a club wanted to try to turn him into a catcher.

Already graduated, Wolf could be an intriguing senior sign for teams that like college pitchers who really understand their craft. The stuff is fringy to average, with a fastball that averages 89 mph and tops out at 93 to go along with a mid-70s slider and a low-80s changeup, all of which he can throw for strikes. His stuff could tick up in a bullpen role.

Baker made an immediate contribution as a freshman at Texas Tech in 2019, starting at shortstop and third base and helping the Red Raiders to their best-ever finish (tied for third) at the College World Series. He moved into the leadoff spot and shifted to the outfield corners last year and has remained there this spring, catalyzing Texas Tech's offense. He batted .440 in the first six weeks of the season before sustaining a quad injury that sidelined him for six games. Baker's best tool is his speed, which earns plus-plus grades from some evaluators and makes him a basestealing threat. He has quick hands and a compact right-handed swing built for consistent line-drive contact. While he has average raw power, he focuses more on getting on base than driving the ball and projects to max out around 10-12 homers per season with wood bats. Though his best positions for becoming a big league regular would be second base or center field, Baker hasn't played them in college. He doesn't have the cleanest infield actions and his fringy to average arm isn't suited for an everyday role at shortstop or third base. He has the quickness to cover the gaps in center, a more viable spot than the outfield corners because his lack of pop doesn't fit the profile there.

Nastrini was a tall and projectable pitcher at Cathedral Catholic High School in San Diego, the same school that has produced arms like Stephen Gonsalves and Brady Aiken. Undrafted, he had little problem making the transition to UCLA's rotation as a freshman, pitching well in his first three appearances before being shut down with thoracic outlet syndrome. He worked his way back to make two postseason starts before throwing very well in the Cape Cod League and served as the Bruins' Saturday starter in the short-lived 2020 season. Issues with command have sidetracked him in 2021, as he lost his rotation spot and had trouble making an impact as a reliever. The 6-foot-3 right-hander has a legitimate four-pitch mix with the prototypical starting pitcher's body. His fastball has averaged around 93 mph in 2021 and touched 98, and like most UCLA pitchers, relies on his four-seamer up in the zone, getting hitters to chase and miss it. His best secondary offering is his changeup, which hitters have a hard time recognizing, thrown with a solid 10-mph differential from his fastball, but with the same arm action. He complements those with an average mid-80s slider and mid-to-upper-70s curveball. On the surface, Nastrini has a repeatable delivery, but struggles with finding the strike zone in the shortened 2020 season (5.7 BB/9) have intensified this year. With his stuff still very good and missing bats, a team is likely to take a chance and try to fix him at the next level, but he's looking for like a future reliever, at best.

Ball State has an early-round arm for the third straight Draft, with McDermott poised to follow 2019 D-backs first-rounder Drey Jameson and 2020 Marlins second-rounder Kyle Nicolas. He redshirted in 2017 while recovering from Tommy John surgery and missed the final six weeks of the 2019 season with an injury, but he started to break out last year with 11 strikeouts in six no-hit innings against Richmond in his final start. He continued to impress in the Grand Park Summer League and has added more velocity this spring. After working at 90-95 mph in the past, McDermott has sat at 92-96 and reached 98 with his four-seam fastball in 2021 while showing the ability to carry mid-90s velocity for several innings at a time. He's still seeking consistency with his secondary pitches, highlighted by an upper-70s curveball with downer break that can be a plus pitch at times but difficult to land at others. He also has a decent mid-80s changeup. A quality athlete who comes from a basketball family that includes a brother (Sean) on the NBA's Memphis Grizzlies, McDermott has cleaned up his delivery and made progress with his control while at Ball State. He still needs to improve his ability to locate his pitches, especially his curve. He has a chance to make it as a starter in pro ball but also could move quickly as a reliever.

A fifth-year senior who started his career at Grayson (Texas) CC, Glenn improved his quickness and athleticism before his final season. He shifted from third base to second base and won Missouri Valley Conference Player of the Year honors, hitting .364/.435/.716 while leading NCAA Division I in total bases (172) and ranking fourth in homers (21). He has a short right-handed stroke, perhaps solid raw power, average speed and gets the job done at the keystone.

Two years after producing a first-round pick in Daniel Espino, Georgia Premier Academy has two more draftable pitchers in Bratt and Erian Rodriguez. A Canadian who came south for his senior season, Bratt is a projectable left-hander who won't turn 18 until a week before the Draft. He impressed pitching against older competition in the inaugural season of the MLB Draft League. Bratt attacks hitters with an 88-90 mph fastball that tops out at 92 but works because it has good carry at the top of strike zone and he commands it well. He shows promising feel for spin with a mid-70s curveball that should become a solid pitch once he adds more strength. He also displays aptitude for throwing a low-80s changeup with depth, though it can get too firm at times. Bratt has an efficient delivery with little effort and repeats it well, allowing him to throw strikes with all of his offerings. The Florida State recruit stands out with his mound presence and has a higher floor than a typical prep pitcher. He could become a No. 4 starter if his stuff picks up as he physically matures.

Kohlhepp began his college career at Tennessee in 2019 but worked just four innings because he couldn't throw strikes, and he continued to struggle with his control at Iowa Western CC last spring. He has been a different pitcher at Notre Dame in 2021, helping the Fighting Irish reach the NCAA Division I super regionals for the first time in 19 years. He has been a weapon as a resilient multi-inning arm out of the bullpen and could get a chance to start in pro ball. Kohlhepp's fastball sits at 92-96 mph and reaches 99 with armside run and sink that generates weak ground-ball contact more than swings and misses. He destroys right-handers with a tight low-80s slider that could be a back-foot option against left-handers, and he'll sometimes turn it into an upper-80s cutter. He prefers to attack lefties with a mid-80s changeup that has nifty fade when it's on. With three pitches and improved control, Kohlhepp merits a look as a starter at the next level. He's doing a better job of repeating his deceptive delivery and maintains his stuff for several innings at a time. He needs to refine his command, though the life on his pitches helps him avoid barrels.

Hickey attended Providence High School in Jacksonville, playing with Reds 2019 third-round pick Tyler Callihan. His left-handed bat garnered some attention, but he went undrafted and went on to the University of Florida. He made an immediate offensive impact as a freshman in the shortened 2020 season and has kept on swinging the bat well as a Draft-eligible sophomore to put an up arrow next to his name on Draft boards. Hickey has raised his offensive profile to the point where he's now being considered to be one of the best bats in Florida. He has a solid approach at the plate, drawing a ton of walks. He's been tapping into his power and while some scouts see a bit of a max effort swing, he's cut his strikeout rate down considerably this year. Hickey lost 20-25 pounds when he first got to Florida and has kept the weight off, making him more athletic in the box. The bat is going to have to play because few scouts believe he'll be able to catch long-term. He has more than enough arm for the position, but lacks the agility or the hands to deal with high-octane pitching. The best possible defensive outcome might be for him to move to left field and let the bat carry him to the big leagues in a Kyle Schwarber type of trajectory.

Though he has been a right fielder for most of his college career, Roberts has experience as a catcher and scouts are intrigued with the idea of putting him behind the plate in pro ball. He's a left-handed hitter with some patience, power and solid arm strength. He batted .270/.442/.476 with 10 homers in 55 games this spring.

An eighth-round pick in the 2016 Ontario Hockey League draft, Ceranatola was the top Canadian pitching prospect in the 2018 Draft but slid to the Rays in the 30th round because of signability and headed to Mississippi State. After pitching sparingly in his first two years with the Bulldogs, he created first-round buzz by showcasing perhaps better stuff than any college pitcher anywhere last fall. But he pitched himself out of Mississippi State's rotation a month into the 2021 season, barely saw action afterward and was left off the College World Series roster of the eventual national champions.  During fall practice, Cerantola pushed his electric fastball to 98 mph and his curveball up to 87 with an astounding 3300 rpm. He averaged 95 mph and hit 100 with his heater this spring, but he struggled to throw it for strikes and it got hit when he dialed it back to find the zone. His curve remained devastating if not quite as powerful. Though Cerantola doesn't use his third pitch much, he does show some feel for a low-80s changeup with fade. But he can't succeed in the rotation if he can't dramatically improve his control and command, and the effort and stiffness in his delivery make it difficult to do so. He has frontline starter upside but comes with a lot of reliever risk and Mississippi State didn't trust him in high-leverage situations.

Justice helped North Carolina State to a third-place finish at the College World Series by saving 13 games (fourth in NCAA Division I) and logging a 3.77 ERA with 74 strikeouts in 59 2/3 innings. He has a 93-95 mph fastball that reaches 98 and a sharp low-80s slider, though he's purely a reliever and sometimes battles the strike zone.

Calvary Christian High School in Fort Lauderdale has a pair of top Draft prospect arms this year in Andrew Painter, a first-round talent, and Irving Carter. Three years ago, Scott was a bit of an off-the-radar pitcher there, with a University of Florida commitment. He went undrafted and has spent three years pitching effectively, largely out of the bullpen, for the Gators. A 6-foot-4 right-hander, Scott had a lot of success in 2021 leaning mostly on his fastball and slider. He's touched 98 mph with his heater and is regularly up to 95 mph, rarely thrown straight. He throws his mid-80s slider almost as much as his fastball and he does miss a fair amount of bats with it and is most successful when he stays down in the zone. He had a curve and changeup when he first arrived on campus and while he does mix in the occasional offspeed offering, he's largely shelved them. Scott threw a ton of strikes in 2021, though he's more control over command with a head whack and some effort in his delivery limiting him somewhat. He's limited to a bullpen role, but he's shown his stuff should be effective with a similar gig at the next level.

White's biggest backers believe the switch-hitter has some of the best raw power among high school players in the Southeast. The Indiana recruit has a quick bat and a strong 6-foot-2, 210-pound frame. There are some questions about his pure hitting ability and his chances of staying in the outfield rather than moving to first base.

A .296/.397/.429 hitter with 10 steals this spring, Murray stands out more for his instincts than his physical ability and projects as a utility man at the big league level. He's overly aggressive with a right-handed swing that gets too long and has fringy speed, with his solid arm grading as his lone better than average tool

Allen's ability to do a little bit of everything combined with his instincts and hustle made him an immediate starter at South Carolina and have endeared him to scouts during his three years with the Gamecocks. He reached double figures in homers and steals by mid-May and could have average tools across the board if he makes adjustments at the plate. He's a gut-feel guy for enough clubs that he could fit in the first five rounds. A rare bats-right, throws-left player, Allen should be able to hit for average with his swing, feel for hitting and ability to handle both velocity and breaking balls. He hasn't done so consistently in college, however, because he makes too much groundball contact and struggles against changeups. His plus raw power translates into average home run production, and it's playing better to all fields in 2021. Allen has average speed but plays faster than that on the bases and in center field because of his quick first step and his instincts. He's an average defender in center field and solid in right field, where he has just enough arm strength. He might be more of a platoon player or a fourth outfielder, though he has the makeup to exceed expectations.

Farr would have been one of the first junior college pitchers drafted in 2019 had he not injured his shoulder at Northwest Florida State JC, limiting him to 13 innings and dropping him to the Rangers in the 37th round. After he transferred to South Carolina, the shortened 2020 season didn't afford him much opportunity to shake the tag that he was a future reliever. He has done so this spring, pitching well in duels against projected early first-round picks Ty Madden (Texas) and Kumar Rocker (Vanderbilt) while establishing himself as the Palmetto State's best college prospect. Farr is doing a better job of maintaining his fastball velocity, working at 93-95 mph and topping out at 97 while using his high three-quarters arm slot to create downhill plane. He has found a better breaking ball in a spike curve he learned during the pandemic layoff, a low-80s offering that's effective as a chase pitch but sometimes tricky to land for strikes. He also has feel for a low-80s changeup with sink.  Known for the recoil and substantial effort in his delivery in junior college, Farr has cleaned that up with the Gamecocks and now looks more balanced and online to the plate. He has a better chance of remaining in the rotation as a pro, though he's still a 6-foot right-hander and his control and command never have been his strengths. If he ends up in the bullpen, he has the stuff and mentality to handle a late-inning role.

Villanova might be more known for its basketball program, but it has had more than 60 baseball players drafted in the program's history, though none since 2017 and the last big leaguer to come from the school was outfielder Matt Szczur, a 2010 draftee. Graceffo, who worked his way into the Wildcats' weekend rotation as a freshman in 2019, had taken a nice step forward as a starting pitcher prospect before the shutdown in 2020 and carried that over to 2021, where his dominance as the school's Friday night starter gives him the chance to be just the second Villanova product to go in the top seven rounds since Szczur was selected in the fifth. After a big winter workout regimen, Graceffo showed up this spring throwing harder than he had previously, with his fastball now up to 95 mph. He throws it with good sink and that, plus a changeup that could be a plus pitch in the future, elicits a lot of weak contact on the ground. He has an average slider that averages around 81 mph and he can subtract from it to give it more of a mid-70s curve type feel. With the uptick in velocity, the 6-foot-4 Graceffo has actually become a better strike-thrower this spring while missing more bats. He won't get out-worked in the weight room and gets high marks for his competitive nature on the mound, ingredients teams love in a college performer.

Teams that have to play Calvary Christian Academy in Florida might think they'd be lucky if they didn't have to face the school's ace and potential top 10 pick, Andrew Painter. They'd be wrong, as the school's No. 2 starter, Carter, would be the top guy in most other high school programs and is a legitimate Draft prospect in his own right. There are a lot of things to like with Carter, particularly his size, physicality and arm strength. The 6-foot-4 right-hander has a fastball that is currently up to 94 mph, but given his frame, scouts feel there's more in the tank, with a consistent mid-90s fastball certainly feasible. He complements his fastball with a very good slider, thrown in the low 80s. He hasn't needed it much, but Carter has shown some feel for a changeup, though it can get firm at times, up to 87 mph. Carter will change up his delivery, incorporating things like a Johnny Cueto-like turn-around, but he tends to be around the plate and should have average control once he commits to a consistent delivery. Given his size and stuff, the Miami recruit has reminded some of Touki Toussaint, who was a first-round pick from the area back in 2014.

A Rockies 37th-round pick out of a California high school in 2017, Hardman starred in the Cape Cod League two summers later, winning all-star recognition and the home run derby while ranking third with eight homers in 44 games. But he didn't get a chance to build on that momentum during the shortened college season in 2020, when he went unselected in the five-round Draft. He used his pandemic downtime to get into better shape, which has paid off with him becoming one of the best all-around offensive players and the batting champion (.397) in the Big 12 Conference this spring. After dropping 20 pounds, Hardman is displaying better bat speed from the right side of the plate. He tracks balls well, hits high velocity as well as offspeed pitches and draws walks because he isn't afraid to work deep counts (which also leads to strikeouts). With his quicker bat to go with his strength and the leverage in his 6-foot-3 frame, he's better able to translate his at least plus raw power into home run production to all fields. Though he's moving better than ever, Hardman is still a below-average runner who may be limited to first base. He needs to clean up his footwork but projects as an average defender with arm strength to match. He played mostly third base on the Cape but would have a long ways to go to become adequate at the hot corner, while left field could be a more realistic option for expanding his defensive horizons.

An Australian whose older brother Josh pitched in the big leagues, Spence helped Central Arizona CC win the 2019 Junior College World Series and sparked Tennessee to the 2021 College World Series in his lone full season in Knoxville. He batted .336/.472/.459, led the Southeastern Conference in on-base percentage and ranked fifth in NCAA Division I with 54 walks with a patient, contact-oriented approach that yields little power from the right side of the plate. While he's older than most Draftees at 23 years and three months, his on-base ability, plus speed and ability to make routine plays at shortstop make him one of the better discount prospects available.

McDougal's father, Mike, played six seasons of Minor League ball and attends the same Las Vegas area high school as fellow '21 Draft prospect Chris Cortez. McDougal has arm strength and projection in his 6-foot-5 frame, with a fastball typically up to 93-94 mph, though he was in the mid-90s during a Draft combine performance that helped his stock. He showed off premium spin rates on his breaking stuff at the combine, though with a little lower slot, his slider and curve can run into each other at tims. He's committed to the University of Oregon.

Cal State Fullerton has a well-deserved reputation for developing starting pitchers with extreme strike-throwing ability, with arms like Thomas Eshelman (0.4 BB/9 in college) and Connor Seabold (1.4 BB/9) the standard bearers, both of whom went in the top three rounds of their respective Drafts. Bibee is trying to follow in their footsteps after becoming a full-time starter as a sophomore in 2019, and while he pitched very well in four outings as the Friday night starter in 2020, he went unselected in the shortened Draft and returned as the Titans' ace in 2021. Bibee has continued to be stingy with the bases on balls, filling the zone with a solid -- albeit unspectacular -- three-pitch mix. His fastball has added a tick more velocity and now sits in the 90-92 mph range while touching 94, and he'll mix in both a two- and a four-seam fastball. He'll show two breaking balls and has favored his curve more this year than his slider compared to 2020. He also factors in a usable changeup. All of his stuff plays up because he commands three pitches extremely well and has a good understanding of how to game plan and keep hitters guessing. Like with the Fullerton starters who preceded him, Bibee's ceiling is somewhat limited, but he has a similar chance to be a back-end starter in the big leagues if it all clicks.

Scouts have had interest in Waddell in the previous Drafts but he didn't make himself signable enough to get picked higher than the 32nd round (Diamondbacks) in 2019 or selected in the five-round Draft a year ago. One of the best contact hitters in college baseball, he had nearly twice as many walks (30) as strikeouts (16) while batting .309/.402/.474 this spring. A left-handed hitter, he has added some strength this year but still is a hit-over-power guy with solid speed and arm strength.

With Ty Duvall and Dominic Keegan sidelined at the start of the 2020 season, Rodriguez suddenly became Vanderbilt's regular catcher as a freshman. He showed the ability to handle college baseball's most talented pitching staff and has boosted his profile by providing more offense this spring. Most teams project him as a quality backup, though a club that thinks he can hit enough to be regular could take him in the top four rounds. Rodriguez has soft hands and does a nice job of receiving and blocking pitches, and he moves fine behind the plate despite bottom-of-the-scale speed. His arm earns average-to-solid grades and plays up because he makes accurate throws. He has strong leadership skills and earns praise for his ability to manage a game and his pitchers. Rodriguez focuses on making contact with a simple right-handed swing. He's rarely fooled at the plate, controls the strike zone and hits the ball where it's pitched. He's walking more often and driving the ball more consistently in 2021 after rarely doing so a year ago, but he still figures to top out at 10-12 home runs annually.

Though the pandemic limited him to just four starts last spring, MacLeod tied a Mississippi State freshman record with a pair of double-digit strikeout outings, matching former first-round choices Eric DuBose and Paul Maholm. He fanned 45 percent of the hitters he faced in his debut, which was delayed a year because he redshirted in 2019 after coming down with pneumonia. He continued to miss bats during the 2021 regular season but struggled terribly in the postseason, including getting shelled in both of his College World Series starts despite the Bulldogs winning their first national title. MacLeod's best pitch is a plus upper-70s curveball with good depth that plays well off his fastball. His heater has below-average velocity at 87-93 mph but is effective because he commands it, tunnels it well with his curve and it has high spin rates and riding life that keep it off barrels. He also has the makings of at least an average changeup in the low 80s with sink. MacLeod uses his 6-foot-4 frame and high three-quarters arm slot to create downhill plane on his pitches and good extension in his delivery, which he repeats easily. His strength, durability and efficiency with locating his offerings all enhance his chances at making a big league rotation. He keeps hitters off balance with his ability to sequence his pitches and move them around the strike zone.

The Volunteers have their deepest group of position players in years and Ferguson entered 2021 considered as their best hitting prospect since Nick Senzel, the No. 2 overall pick in 2016. Viewed as similar to Senzel as a hit-over-power guy with the tools to play all over the diamond, Ferguson had a chance to climb into the first round. While he helped Tennessee reach the College World Series for the first time since 2005, he had a mystifying season as he lost weight and changed his approach at the plate. In the past, Ferguson displayed a sweet left-handed swing, controlled the strike zone extremely well and barreled balls with ease. Though his strength and bat speed decreased this spring, he also looked to drive the ball in the air to his pull side much more often than in the past. He did produce more home runs and continued to draw walks, but he also made less contact, had trouble catching up to quality fastballs and struggled against sliders. With speed that consistently plays at plus and flashes better than that, as well as good instincts on the bases, Ferguson is a threat to steal. He's a smooth defender at second base and turns double plays well. He has the actions to play at shortstop and his internal clock might make up for his average arm there, and he definitely has the quickness to handle center field if desired.

The University of Connecticut has produced a good amount of big league arms over the years, with former first-round pick Matt Barnes leading the more recent crop and Jacob Wallace and Tim Cate having gone in the top three rounds over the last few Drafts. Casparius, a Connecticut native who spent two years hitting and pitching at North Carolina before transferring to UConn, might not go quite that highly, but his combination of stuff and relatively fresh arm intrigued scouts in 2021. While not the biggest guy in the world at 6 feet, Casparius is strong and athletic and has the chance to have a legitimate three-pitch mix. His fastball sits around 91 mph in most starts, but he'll touch 94-95 on occasion. He can miss bats with his low-80s slider, a breaking ball with good hard, short action to it, but it's his changeup that's his best pitch, an offspeed offering also in the low 80s that features good fade, can serve as a strikeout pitch and gets weak contact on the ground. He's generally a strike-thrower, with a couple of bad outings upping his walk rate this year, and his simple and repeatable delivery point to good command in the future. Prior to starting for UConn, Casparius had thrown just 27 innings, so there isn't a lot of mileage on his arm. That also means there's not a ton of history on him, though the Huskies helped offset that with a very scout-accessible fall program. He's been a consistent member of their weekend rotation this spring.

After batting just .243/.373/.320 in his first two seasons at Tulane, Burns added strength during the pandemic layoff and jumped to .353/.410/.571 with 20 steals this spring. A left-handed hitter with plus speed, good hands and average arm strength, he profiles best as an offensive second baseman in pro ball.

Kent State is an underrated pitching factory that produced six arms taken in the top five rounds over the previous decade, including big leaguers Andrew Chafin, Eric Lauer and Taylor Williams. Albright should be the next in line after making a successful transition from relieving in 2019 to starting last spring, allowing a total of five earned runs in four outings. He continued to pitch well in the Grand Park Summer League and again this year despite taking a line drive off his pitching arm in March, adding to what should be Ohio's strongest college pitching crop in years. Albright's best pitch is his breaking ball, though there's some disagreement as to which one. He generates high spin rates on an upper-70s curveball with good shape, while some scouts prefer his sharper low-80s slider that he also can morph into a mid-80s cutter. His low-90s fastball plays up because he uses his 6-foot-4 frame and high three-quarters arm slot to create downhill plane. Albright also possesses a reliable changeup with some sink, giving him four average or better offerings. How high he'll go in the Draft will depend on his ability to throw strikes, which improved in 2020 after he battled the zone during his freshman season. His competitiveness adds to his No. 4 starter upside.

Dooney began his college career at the University of Arizona and had a very strong freshman year for the Wildcats, hitting .323/.417/.596 in 2019. He didn't replicate that success in the shortened 2020 season, then decided to transfer to Central Arizona Junior College for the 2021 season. The infielder hit .376/.452/.706 and helped Central Arizona reach the championship game of the Junior College World Series, earning a spot on the all-tournament team. A switch-hitter, Dooney has a solid approach and some bat speed from both sides of the plate. He has an advanced feel to hit and doesn't strike out much, typically turning in very competitive at-bats. There's some sneaky power, especially to the pull side. He's not a runner and it was even harder to evaluate that part of his game as he played through a hamstring injury for much of the 2021 season. That also made it tougher to grade out his defense since he couldn't move laterally very well, but the hope is he could be average with the glove at second, his best chance to be a regular at one spot. It will be the bat that teams will be buying with the hope he can develop into an offensive-minded second baseman at the next level.

Seymour has some of the best pure arm strength in the Draft, featuring a mid-90s fastball that reaches triple digits and an upper-80s slider that climbs into the 90s. A fourth-year collegian who spent his freshman season at Dartmouth, he never has harnessed his stuff to miss bats or throw strikes consistently and almost certainly will be a reliever rather than a starter at the next level.

One of the best left-handed relievers available in the 2020 Draft, Kirian turned down overtures from pro clubs, went unselected and became a starter for Louisville in his fourth college season. He'll probably return to the bullpen in pro ball after showing a low-90s fastball and solid slurve in that role that backed up a bit when he moved to the rotation. He uses his 6-foot-6 frame to create difficult angle for hitters, especially lefties.

Over the summer, Pena showed off some premium arm strength at events like the East Coast Pro Showcase and in the fall at Perfect Game's World Wood Bat Association World Championship. He hasn't missed a step this spring in terms of lighting up radar guns, bringing scouts to Tampa Prep all season. The 6-foot-3 Pena relies heavily on his fastball and he can crank it up to 95-96 mph at times. He missed a lot of bats with his heater at showcases, though scouts were concerned it was a little too true and hittable this spring. His mid-70s curveball has improved this spring and he throws both his fastball and breaking ball with a quick arm, posting high spin rates. He does have a seldom-used changeup, around 78-80 mph. Pena generally is around the zone, though he can struggle landing his secondary stuff for strikes, and he's definitely control over command right now. Still his size, arm strength and athleticism on the mound could lead teams to take a look at him on Day 2 and try to keep him from attending Florida International in the fall.

Lonsway was a prime Draft prospect in both 2017, when he was Ohio's top high schooler and fell to the 19th round, and in 2020, when he led NCAA Division I in strikeout rate (21.0 per nine innings) as a redshirt sophomore at Ohio State but went unpicked. Academically ineligible because of a transcript snafu in 2018, he's now 22 and in his fourth year of college, so his negotiating leverage is diminishing. He has one of the best curveballs in this year's Draft and can look like a first-rounder on his best days -- such as when he struck out 17 with just one walk against Indiana in early April -- but his inability to throw strikes makes him almost certainly destined for the bullpen in pro ball. Lonsway's curve has quality power (low 80s) and depth that earn it comparisons to Barry Zito's bender when at its best. Both his 91-96 mph four-seam fastball and his mid-80s slider/cutter can flash as plus pitches as well. He shows some feel for a changeup though he doesn't incorporate it very often. While Lonsway can land his curveball for strikes, his control and command are serious concerns that figure to make him a reliever in the long run. He doesn't locate his fastball well and has averaged more than six walks per nine innings between Ohio State and two summers in the Cape Cod League. He has an odd delivery that features an extremely high arm slot and severe head tilt, and he struggles to keep his mechanics in sync.

Thompson drew little scouting or recruiting interest as a Tampa high schooler and was a part-timer as a Northwest Florida State JC freshman in 2019 before attracting some attention by homering three times in the state juco tournament. The shortened 2020 season and struggles at the plate last fall after transferring to North Carolina did little to enhance his profile, but he changed that in his Atlantic Coast Conference debut in February. He had a homer, a double, a bunt single, a steal and two spectacular catches in front of a large contingent of scouts, one of whom described it as a Byron Buxton-esque performance. Thompson comes with concerns about his bat but he has some of the best tools in the college class. He's a well above-average runner who's aggressive on the bases and covers a lot of ground in center field, where he's a plus defender with an average arm. He also features plus raw power, though oddly enough it's much more apparent in games than in batting practice, when he often hits without striding. On pure physical ability, Thompson would go in the first two rounds, but scouts don't like his spread-out stance and long right-handed stroke. He shows some patience at the plate, though analytically minded clubs worry about his propensity to swing and miss (especially against fastballs) and note that he produces good but not great exit velocities. He has made some progress with his swing and his ability to deal with breaking balls, but there's risk that he maxes out as a platoon player or fourth outfielder.

Used almost exclusively as a reliever at Missouri State, Juenger worked just 21 innings this spring and left scouts wishing they could have seen more of him. Several evaluators believe he can start in pro ball because he has a mid-90s fastball that tops out at 98 and backs it up with an average slider and changeup. He also has a rotation-worthy delivery and throws plenty of strikes.

Fitts was a key reliever for Auburn as a freshman in 2019, throwing six shutout innings to beat Georgia Tech in the NCAA regional playoffs, winning the clincher against North Carolina in the super regionals and pitching well in two College World Series appearances. He remained in the bullpen during the shortened 2020 season but raised his profile significantly as a starter during summer play and fall practice. Viewed as a potential first-rounder, he instead has become an enigma after sustaining a foot injury in early March and pitching sparingly afterward. After operating with a low-90s sinker as a reliever, Fitts began employing a four-seam fastball as a starter. He showed the ability to sit at 93-95 mph for several innings and reach 97 with quality vertical life on his heater last year and has mostly held that velocity in 2021. He has two promising secondary pitches, with his upper-80s changeup with some splitter action currently a more reliable option than his improving mid-80s slider, but both have regressed this spring. Fitts' stuff has gotten better as he has grown into his 6-foot-3 frame, which is strong enough for him to handle a starter's workload. His arm action and delivery are sound, and he throws strikes with his entire arsenal. Adding to the package is his competitiveness, which draws as much praise as his stuff.

A reserve on Vanderbilt's 2019 College World Series championship team, Malloy played sparingly in two years with the Commodores before transferring to Georgia Tech and batting .308/.436/.558 with 11 homers this season. He has bat speed and strength but scouts don't love his right-handed swing and worry about his ability to hit breaking balls. He's a solid defender at the hot corner.

During his first two years at California, Holman had done double-duty as a pitcher and hitter, contributing as a weekend starter and as a first baseman/DH during the shortened 2020 season. The right-hander threw well in the San Diego League this past summer and showed off improved stuff in the fall. Scouts had to wait to see if it carried over to the spring as Holman missed the start of 2021, but came back in late March with similarly electric stuff. Holman has the chance to have a legitimate four-pitch mix, all coming from a strong, durable build that points to a potential starting pitching profile. He touched 97 mph as a reliever in San Diego, and was 93-97 mph out of the gate this spring for Cal, throwing his fastball with heavy riding life. He already had a live slider and a splitter he uses as his offspeed offering and added a slower 12-to-6 curve that looks like it could be a usable weapon, while the slider and splitter both can miss bats. All of Holman's pitches have a lot of action and he has a feel to find the strike zone with all of them. There's not a lot of track record yet given his split focus, but he is a college arm with some serious upside, one who could climb boards in a hurry as a full-time starter this spring.

The 6-foot-5 right-hander, who pitched in Boston College's weekend rotation in 2021, is a bit raw, but has a three-pitch mix and showed he can miss bats, striking out 12.4 per nine this spring. His fastball sat around 91-92 mph, but he often reached back for 95-96 mph, complementing the heater with a mid-70s curveball and an upper-70s changeup. He'll need to keep refining his command to have a chance to start at the next level.

Hurter looked like he was headed for the top three rounds of the 2020 Draft as a Georgia Tech sophomore the previous spring, then blew out his elbow that April and required Tommy John surgery. He missed all of last season before returning in 2021, showing similar stuff to what he had previously but not missing as many bats. He still comes with a high floor as a potential No. 5 starter. While Hurter's fastball usually ranges from 88-92 mph and tops out at 94, it plays better than its velocity and elicits a lot of weak ground-ball contact because he sinks it and locates it well. His best pitch is a sharp 78-82 mph slider that shuts down left-handers and is effective against right-handers. He throws his changeup at the same velocity and it shows flashes of becoming a solid offering with some tumble. Hurter has cleaned up his delivery a bit since having his elbow reconstructed and continues to pound the strike zone, though he gets in trouble when his command wavers because he has little margin for error. He has good feel for mixing his pitches and uses his 6-foot-6 frame to create angle and plane. If he doesn't make it as a starter, he could be a reliever who relies on his slider.

Shawver's stock took a hit when he missed five starts with shoulders this spring, but it's on the rise again after he struck out 22 in 12 innings without a walk or an earned run in his first two outings in the Cape Cod League. When he's at his best, his fastball reaches 96 mph with riding action, his slider can be a plus pitch and his changeup can neutralize right-handers.

After missing the shortened 2020 season with an injury, Kendall returned to star atop UCLA's lineup in 2021. The big key to his success this spring was a vastly improved approach at the plate and large decrease in his whiff rate as a result, hitting for averag and getting on base, where he could use his excellent speed more frequently. He's also shown defensive flexibility in his career with the Bruins, playing center field as well as both middle infield positions.

A plus runner who knows how to steal a base, Young is an outstanding athlete who has a solid approach at the plate and can spray the ball to all fields. There isn't a ton of pop, but there's some extra-base authority for doubles. He played left in deference to Jud Fabian in 2021 but has the skills to play center, while some scouts like him even more at second base, the position he played as a freshman for the Gators in 2019.

McGowan drew scouting interest in 2020, when he ranked fourth among national junior college pitchers with 58 strikeouts in 35 1/3 innings, but ultimately went unpicked. He initially planned on transferring to Kansas State for 2021 but opted to return to Eastern Oklahoma State JC, where he's part of a rotation that could have three members selected in single-digit rounds this July. While Mountaineers left-hander Andrew Walling is ahead of McGowan in MLB Pipeline's Draft rankings, the latter has more athleticism and room to add polish. McGowan operates at 92-94 mph and reaches 99 with his fastball, which features more downhill plane than true life. He gets depth on an 82-84 mph slider that peaks at 88 with bite when it's on but gets slurvy when it's not. His mid-80s changeup has nice fade and shows flashes of becoming at least a solid pitch at its best, though it gets too firm and he struggles to throw it for strikes. While he's naturally athletic, McGowan throws with effort and recoil in his three-quarters delivery, which compromises his ability to control and command his arsenal and also casts doubt on his long-term future as a starter. One scout likened him to Trevor Rosenthal when the future All-Star was at Cowley County (Kan.) CC. Clubs could get a better read on McGowan if he attends Texas Tech in 2022, but his arm talent may be too enticing to pass up this summer.

If it seems like Abel has been on the amateur prospect radar for a very long time, you aren't wrong. The right-hander snuck on to the back end of MLB Pipeline's Draft Top 200 as a right-hander with a good feel for pitching from the Southern California prep ranks back in 2017, though the industry knew he was keen on honoring his commitment to Oregon State. He starred in the College World Series as a freshman in 2018, then made only three starts in 2019 and didn't return before the shutdown in 2020. He was Oregon State's Friday starter in 2021. In many ways, Abel is the same pitcher he was entering college in terms of his all-around feel with a solid three-pitch mix. He generally throws his fastball up to 91 mph, occasionally touching 92, but sitting more often than not in the 88- to 90-mph range while showing the ability to command it to both sides of the plate. His changeup, a plus offspeed offering, is his best secondary pitch and he'll throw his above-average curve at any point in the count. For someone who has the reputation for having such an advanced feel for pitching, Abel has at times struggled with throwing consistent strikes, with a career walk rate over 5.0 per nine innings -- perhaps a result of him nibbling too much. He does mix his pitches well and keeps hitters off balance with his consistent performance, pointing to a future in the back end of a rotation.

Regis Jesuit High School in Colorado has built itself into a baseball power in the state and has had 10 players drafted since 2009. Only one signed out of high school (Max George) and one has made it to the big leagues after heading to college (David Peterson of the Mets). Because Willis was hurt last year, he didn't participate in events last summer, but he was a bit of a pop-up guy this spring who had some helium as the Draft approached. Willis is all about projection. Much of that stems from his 6-foot-5 frame that makes it easy to dream there's more velocity to come. As it is, he sits in the 90-94 mph range, maintaining his velocity well, throwing his fastball with some life. He'll flash a solid average curveball and does show feel for a changeup with decent arm speed and some sink and fade. A lack of track record against top competition, as well as spotty control and command, are the things that might hold Willis back come Draft time. He's committed to the University of Minnesota and should he head to school, it's easy to believe he'll be a much more heralded prospect for the 2024 Draft.

After hitting 22 homers and stealing 19 bases in his first two seasons at Coastal Carolina and performing well in the Cape Cod League, Chavers positioned himself as a potential top-two-rounds pick for 2020. But he never made his way back to the diamond after surgery to repair a torn labrum in his right (throwing) shoulder in December 2019 and went undrafted last June. He's again displaying some of the best all-around tools in the college crop and a more mature approach at the plate, but worries about his shoulder and his age (he turns 23 two weeks after the Draft) will affect his status. Chavers generates at least solid raw power with a quick left-handed swing and the strength in his compact frame, and he's not swinging for the fences as much as he did in the past. He used to let his stroke get too uphill and pull-oriented, but now he's making more consistent hard contact. While he has made strides handling left-handers, questions still persist as to whether he can do so well enough to be more than a platoon player. Chavers is doing a better job of using his plus speed to steal and take extra bases. He's still honing his instincts in center field but should be able to remain there as a pro. Though his arm earns average to solid grades, it remains a concern because he had labrum surgery in high school.

Leftwich has intrigued with his arm strength since his high school days in Florida and was a late-round pick by the Tigers out of TNXL Academy in 2017, though it was common knowledge he was headed to pitch for the University of Florida. He's both started and relieved since joining the Gators' staff in 2018, showing glimpses of power stuff in both roles. He began the 2021 season throwing well as part of the weekend rotation, then showed how dominant he could be during some multi-inning relief outings in April. The 6-foot-2 right-hander has largely been a two-pitch guy, though those two pitches have been very good. He'll crank his fastball up to 96 mph regularly, sitting around 94 mph, though it can be a bit true at times. He'll miss bats with his slider, which flashes plus, but he does struggle landing it within the strike zone. He does have a changeup, but it's not nearly as good as his other offerings. Because of the two-pitch mix, the spotty command and the bulldog mentality on the mound, many scouts feel Leftwich is better suited to a relief role at the next level. A team taking him in the top five rounds could send him out as a starter to see how his stuff develops, knowing that shortening him up and letting him rely on that fastball-slider combination could get him to the big leagues quickly.

Part of a Colleyville Heritage program that won a Texas 5-A state title with No. 2 overall pick Bobby Witt in 2019, Smith-Shawver initially was better known for his football exploits. A three-star quarterback recruit who accounted for 29 touchdowns in nine games last fall, he has a brighter future as a pitcher. He has committed to Texas Tech for baseball and may get the opportunity to play football there should he attend college.  Smith-Shawver barely pitched at Colleyville Heritage before this spring but attracted attention on the showcase circuit last summer with his strong 6-foot-3 frame and a fastball that reached 95 mph. He has pitched in the low 90s as a senior with some armside run on his heater. He has made encouraging progress with his curveball, improving its shape while still needing to add more power because it parks in the mid-70s. Because of his inexperience, Smith-Shawver is still raw on the mound. He has a methodical delivery and could add some more power to his pitches if he throws with more intent. He has barely begun working on a changeup that he doesn't use in games, but his athleticism should help him get better once he fully devotes himself to baseball.

The Gonzaga senior was the West Coast Conference Defensive Player of the Year and was named to the all-conference first team. He can play good defense at both short and third and took a step forward offensively in 2021, hitting for average (.355) and rarely striking out (10.1 percent K rate).

A member of the Goodlettsville (Tenn.) team that won the U.S. championship at the 2012 Little League World Series, Rucker became an immediate starter when he arrived at Tennessee. His power began to emerge with five homers in his first 10 postseason games this year, including a pair of blasts off Louisiana State ace Landon Marceaux in the decisive game of the Knoxville Super Regional. He generates some mixed reviews among scouts but a club that believes he could have average tools across the board could select him as early as the third round. His biggest proponents see Rucker as a hitter who continues to improve his ability to drive the ball to all fields and could provide 15-20 homers per season. Other evaluators question his aggressive approach and low exit velocities, which lead to strikeouts and weak contact. A right-handed hitter, he does a lot of damage against lefties but struggles against breaking pitches. Rucker has more defensive versatility than his fringy speed might indicate, having played second base as a freshman and also seeing action at shortstop before settling in at third base for the Volunteers. He has average hands and arm strength and may profile better at the keystone than the hot corner if his power doesn't develop as hoped. One scout compared him to Jeff Keppinger, albeit with less pure hitting ability.

Workman led the Coastal Plain League in strikeouts (67 in 43 2/3 innings) in the summer of 2019, a prelude to becoming one of the most dominant pitchers in NCAA Division II the last two years. He went 5-0 with a 0.21 ERA, .091 opponent average and 53 whiffs in 42 frames in 2020 and nearly signed with the Yankees as a nondrafted free agent before returning to Lee (Tenn.) for a fourth season. He went 8-1 this spring with a 1.81 ERA, .168 opponent average and 110 strikeouts in 79 2/3 innings while setting a school record for career ERA (2.13). Workman's four-seam fastball reached 98 mph early this season and usually ranges from 91-96 with riding action up in the strike zone. His best secondary pitch is a solid low-80s changeup with fade and sink. He has improved his low-80s slider but it still lacks consistency at times. With his 6-foot-4 frame and high three-quarters arm slot, Workman creates plane on his pitches. He provides strikes but there's some effort in his delivery and he'll need to refine his fastball command to succeed in pro ball. He has the upside of a back-of-the-rotation starter and enough fastball to make it as a reliever if he doesn't stick in the rotation.

Sublette is a fourth-year reliever who produces 92-95 mph fastballs and mid-80s sliders from a deceptive arm slot. He posted a career-best 2.34 ERA this spring with a .183 opponent average and 62 strikeouts in 42 1/3 innings.

As a high-schooler, Sullivan pitched at Chaminade Prep in California and played with former first-rounder Blake Rutherford, pitching for Chaminade en route to a runner-up finish in the 2016 National High School Invitational. He spent most of his freshman year pitching out of Cal's bullpen and then missed the beginning of the shortened 2020 season, returning in time to make two short starts before the shutdown. He's been a mainstay in the weekend rotation this year, showing off an interesting combination of stuff and feel for pitching. At 6-foot-1, Sullivan isn't the biggest right-hander in the world, but he's athletic and has a legitimate four-pitch mix. He mostly pitches with a 90-91 mph fastball, though he can reach back for 94-95 when he needs to. It has small sinking life down in the zone and he can get it to ride when he throws it up. He throws his low-80s slider more than his upper-70s curve, with the former showing good lateral sweep with depth and the chance to be an out pitch and the latter occasionally missing bats but needing some tightening. His changeup features two-plane sink and also misses bats, while he does a good job of getting ground-ball outs as well. Sullivan works fast and competes well on the mound, and should be able to continue throwing strikes as he progresses. He might not have the highest ceiling in the world, but college performers who have the chance to be a No. 4 type starter tend to come off the board on Day 2.

Division II Biola University has had two players make it to the Majors in the program's history and they were brothers. Todd Worrell was a first-rounder in 1982, younger brother Tim went in the 20th round of the 1989 Draft, and combined, they appeared in nearly 1,300 big league games. Kindreich, a big lefty who struggled as a freshman starter, didn't give up an earned run in the shortened 2020 season and pitched well in the Northwoods League that summer to get the chance to be the first player from Biola to go in the top 10 rounds since 2005. Kindreich has been a bit more inconsistent this year than he was in 2020 in either location, but the left-hander could have a three-pitch mix coming from a 6-foot-4 frame. His fastball is typically in the 90-93 mph range, and when he's at his best, he commands it well. He entered the spring with a changeup that was better than his curve, a 78-80 mph offspeed pitch that missed bats, but he focused on his breaking ball, a pitch he sometimes struggles to land for strikes. Both are fringy with the chance to be average offerings in the future. After walking a ton of guys early in his college career, Kindreich took a big step forward with his strike-throwing in 2020, something that's regressed a bit. Even so, his size and left-handedness should get him off the board at some point between when the Worrell brothers were selected.

While he has thrown strikes since he arrived at South Carolina-Upstate from Canada, Marks didn't start to thrive until his velocity picked up in 2020. His stuff has continued to get stronger this spring as he set school records for wins (10) and strikeouts (101 in 95 2/3 innings) in a single season en route to the Big South Conference pitcher of the year award. He'll definitely become the second player in school history selected in the top 10 rounds and could go as high as the first, big leaguer Chad Sobotka, who was a fourth-rounder by the Braves in 2014. Marks' fastball has progressed from averaging 87 mph in 2019 to 90 mph a year ago to 92 mph this season, topping out at 98 as he mixes four-seamers up in the zone and two-seamers down. His heater plays better than its velocity because of his ability to locate it, as does his vastly improved 79-82 mph slider, which can get slurvy but has locked up right-handers in 2021. He has good feel for a fading 79-82 mph changeup that elicits more swings and misses than his other offerings. Marks has a simple, effortless delivery that he repeats with ease, allowing him to put his pitches where he wants and maintain the quality of his stuff deep into games. Though he lacks a plus pitch, there are teams who believe his command could make him a back-of-the-rotation starter. If not, he could carve out a role as a middle reliever who pounds the strike zone.

A year ago, Coby Mayo was a fourth-round draft pick of the Orioles, a third baseman out of Stoneman Douglas High School in Florida. Now it's his friend Conticello's turn, another powerful prep infielder who transferred to Stoneman Douglas for his senior season. While Conticello isn't expected to go as high as Mayo did after struggling a bit this spring, he does possess some similar offensive qualities. At 6-foot-4, the left-handed-hitting Conticello is lean and athletic and like Mayo, has some serious raw power to tap into. It's pop that has shown up occasionally now when he's been on time, but there's more work to do with a swing that has a lot of rotation to it. He can generate bat speed and loft, but also can open up too soon. A solid athlete, Conticello runs well underway, though speed isn't likely to be a part of his game as he matures. A shortstop in high school, no one believes Conticello will stick at the premium position at the next level. Some feel he can handle a move to third, with an arm that works from the left side of the infield, while others feel his footwork will necessitate a move to a corner outfield spot. Wherever he plays, it's the power potential in the bat that will have teams trying to keep him from heading to the University of South Florida in the fall.

A solid high school player in the Tampa area, Martin hit the ground running when he got to Florida State after the Marlins took him in Round 37 of the 2018 Draft. The outfielder was named to multiple Freshman All-American teams after hitting .315/.398/.449 in 2019, and he was batting.324 as a sophomore before the shutdown following an unproductive, albeit brief, turn in the Cape Cod League. Martin made up for some lost time by continuing to hit well in the Florida Collegiate League, hitting .313 over 19 games while playing alongside fellow Florida college outfield prospect Jud Fabian. The left-handed hitter has added a lot of muscle to his 6-foot-3 frame since joining the Seminoles. He's been more hit over power to date, with more doubles pop than over-the-fence power. He does make consistent hard contact and could reach average power in the future. Martin has only manned outfield corners during his time in college, with a slightly above-average arm that plays well in either spot. He lacks the pure speed to play center and scouts aren't sold on his overall defense, thinking left field is likely his best spot at the next level. Behind Adrian Del Castillo and Martin's summer ball teammate Fabian, Martin is the next best college bat in Florida, and it's that bat that will get him drafted in the top few rounds.

At the start of the spring of 2018, Vasil had jumped into first-round conversations as a Boston-area high school pitcher with a ton of upside and projection. An arm issue had slowed him, then he informed teams he didn't want to be drafted so he could head to the University of Virginia. After a rough freshman year, Vasil has been a beacon of consistency in Virginia's rotation, serving as the Saturday starter in 2021. The 6-foot-4 right-hander hasn't exactly developed into the frontline starter teams envisioned, but does have the kind of repertoire and feel for pitching that points to a future in a big league rotation. He'll throw his fastball in the 90-95 mph range, touching the upper ranges early in the game, but mostly settling in around 91-92 mph. He'll throw both a curve and slider, and while some scouts have seen flashes of a plus breaking ball, he typically shows an average ability to spin it. His changeup is probably his best secondary offering, one that is above-average and can serve as an out pitch at times. While Vasil hasn't become the Roger Clemens-redux type pitcher some envisioned, he's greatly improved his strike-throwing ability this year and now looks like a guy with at least three pitches who knows how to use all of them and command them well. That might mean a limited ceiling as a No. 4 or 5 starter, but a team taking him knows what it's getting.

The son of former big leaguer Brian Raabe, Zack batted .463 and led NCAA Division I with 31 hits in 17 games during the shortened 2020 season. He hit .431 in the first month of this season before going into a tailspin and finishing at .315/.407/.532 with six homers in 36 games. Scouts love the righty-swinging Raabe's feel for the barrel and his makeup, but the rest of his tools are fringy and he's limited to second base.

A two-way player in high school, Parks made some intriguing progress on the mound when he focused on pitching as a freshman at Florence-Darlington (S.C.) Technical JC as a freshman in 2020. He received some Draft interest last year that has intensified this spring, when he overcame missing a month due to Covid-19 protocols to pitch the Stingers to the Junior College World Series. He's expected to turn pro rather than follow through on a commitment to South Carolina. Parks has a whippy arm that produces low-90s fastballs that peak at 95 mph, albeit with inefficient spin. His slider shows some promise but often sits in the upper 70s and needs more power. He hasn't had much use for a changeup but shows some feel for the pitch and it features some fade. Parks is athletic and throws strikes but needs to improve his command and add strength to his skinny 6-foot-3 build to succeed at the next level. There's still plenty of room for projection in his frame and the opportunity for development considering he worked just 63 2/3 innings in two seasons of junior college. If everything comes together, he could develop into a three-pitch starter.

Warren broke out in his fourth season at Southeastern Louisiana, going 7-2 with a 2.57 ERA and a 95/25 K/BB ratio in 91 innings. He got stronger and his stuff improved, highlighted by a solid mid-70s curveball and a low-90s fastball that topped out at 98 mph. He also throws a slider and a fading changeup, both in the low 80s, and still has room to add more muscle on his 6-foot-2 frame.

A 27th-round pick by the Indians out of high school in 2017, Opitz is quality receiver with arm strength that grades closer to average but plays as plus because he has a quick transfer and release and makes extremely accurate throws. The switch-hitter projects as more of a backup with limited offensive ceiling after hitting .252/.363/.342 in four seasons with the Razorbacks.

The University of Santa Barbara was a great place for scouts to spend a weekend, and not just because of the location. The Gauchos' weekend rotation featured potential first-round pick Michael McGreevy as well as Boone, a pitchability lefty who doesn't have the same kind of high profile as his rotation-mate, but was still impressing scouts with his ability to dominate opponents. It was Boone, not McGreevy, who was named Big West Co-Pitcher of the Year with Long Beach State's Johnathan Lavallee. Boone has been a starter at Santa Barbara since his freshman year in 2019, showing an ability to miss bats without premium stuff. From a velocity standpoint, his fastball is below-average, topping out at 90 mph and typically sitting in the upper-80s. He commands the pitch well and isn't afraid to come up in the zone with it to get hitters to chase and deception in his delivery allows it to play up. He mixes in a big looping overhand curve that's only thrown in the low-70s, a pitch he's been inconsistent with in terms of landing it for strikes. He sells his 77-79 mph changeup, his best pitch, really well with good arm speed. While Boone's walk rates at Santa Barbara aren't as low as you'd think, scouts have little questions about his strike-throwing ability. His ceiling is limited because of the lack of wow stuff, but a back-end left-handed starter who knows how to keep hitters off-balance will get some looks during Day 2.

Swift went from Arizona high school powerhouse Hamilton straight into Arizona State's lineup as a freshman in 2018, seeing time at both middle infield positions, more at shortstop than second, but not hitting much. His offensive profile didn't improve much as a sophomore as he played second regularly in deference to Alika Williams, a 2020 comp-round pick. Swift hit well in that shortened '20 season and has kept it up this year while manning short every day, greatly improving his Draft stock. There are no questions about Swift's glove. Most scouts see him as a plus defender with outstanding instincts, hands, footwork and enough arm to stay at short for a very long time, and he's already shown he's a plus defender at second should he have to move around the infield at the next level. Whether he'll have to do that or can play shortstop every day at the Major League level will depend on what he can do at the plate. He's definitely improved and was finding a bit more extra-base authority this year, but the question will be just how much he can impact the ball on a consistent basis. He gets high marks for his athleticism and his makeup, giving confidence he'll maximize the tools he does have.

When Cardenas was a high school catcher committed to UCLA, he was known as a glove-first backstop and he went undrafted in 2018, cementing his desire to play for the Bruins. His defense landed him in the starting lineup right out of the gate as a freshman and he hit better than expected, finishing with a .375/.476/.500 line. Cardenas hasn't replicated those offensive gains, either during the shortened 2020 season or in 2021, somewhat limiting his Draft stock. Teams interested in Cardenas will be buying his work behind the plate. He has plus instincts to go along with plus blocking and receiving skills, with outstanding agility. He handles UCLA's pitchers very well and his plus arm has enabled him to throw out more than 50 percent of those who have made the mistake of trying to steal against him. At the plate, Cardenas does have excellent contact skills from the right side of the plate. He's strong, but hasn't shown much power to date and some worry about what kind of offensive impact he'll have at the next level. College backstops who can really catch always garner attention, though Cardenas' limited offensive ceiling will limit how early he goes.

The No. 3 starter on Arkansas' 2019 College World Series team as a freshman, Wicklander struggled to a 6.32 ERA in four starts last year before being diagnosed with diabetes during the pandemic layoff. He opened this season in the Razorbacks bullpen but emerged as the ace of a team ranked No. 1 for most of the season and topped all Southeastern Conference starters in ERA (2.11). He doesn't have the sexiest repertoire but he's a lefty who has proven himself in college baseball's toughest league. Wicklander usually works at 90-92 mph and can reach 96 with his fastball, which plays up because he creates deception with his delivery and it has good carry up in the strike zone. His sweeping 77-81 mph slider shuts down same-side hitters but is just OK against right-handers. He doesn't have much trust in his low-80s changeup, which has some fade when it's on but doesn't land in the zone on a consistent basis. Though Wicklander has less than overpowering stuff, he hides the ball and has a low release height, so hitters don't get good hacks against him. He throws strikes and can use his fastball to get swings and misses in the zone and also as a chase pitch. He profiles as a back-of-the-rotation starter or middle reliever as a pro.

Drafted by the Pirates as a shortstop in the 23rd round in 2018, Miller played the position as a freshman at East Mississippi CC before transferring to Auburn. After starting on the outfield corners for the Tigers in 2020, he has played mostly first base this year while also seeing brief action at short and third base. While his position still needs to be sorted out, he has developed into a dependable power bat in college baseball's toughest conference. Miller has solid strength and bat speed, which combine with the leverage in his left-handed swing to produce 20-homer power, most of which comes to his pull side. He has a sound stroke and good feel for the barrel, and he has a knack for making hard contact against quality fastballs. To become more productive at the plate, he'll need to improve his plate discipline and ability to handle breaking balls and changeups. Miller has played first base this spring more out of team need than any defensive limitations. He moves better than his fringy speed out of the batter's box might indicate and has average arm strength. His best-case scenario is second base and he also could get a look in pro ball at third base or left field.

Higgins attended St. Francis High School in northern California as a high schooler and didn't leave the region when he headed southeast to keep playing at Fresno State. Though he didn't play every day as a freshman, he hit, and continued to do so in his pandemic-shortened sophomore season in 2020. He proved it wasn't a fluke by posting a 1.119 OPS in 2021, giving him the chance to be just the third Bulldogs hitter to be taken in the top 10 rounds since then-catcher Taylor Ward was a first-rounder in 2015. For a spring that had a dearth of college hitters performing well, it was a little bit of a surprise more scouts didn't see Higgins' consistently strong season from the right side of the plate. He's always hit and had big raw power, but with a looser, more flexible body now, he was getting to that power more this season, giving him the chance to hit for both average and some pop at the next level. Higgins has played both third and left field at Fresno State, spending much more time at the latter this spring. He initially looked uncomfortable in both spots, often going to a knee to field a ground ball, but improved as the spring went on. With an above-average arm and decent hands, a team might want to give him a chance on the dirt, but it's the bat that will get him drafted.

Bates has led Louisiana Tech in RBIs for four straight seasons, including 62 in as many games this year while batting .346/.471/.583 and topping Conference USA in on-base percentage. A left-handed hitter, he provides decent hitting ability, pop, speed and defense.

After a solid career at De La Salle High School in northern California, Kafka travelled north and immediately made an impact on Oregon's pitching staff, making 16 appearances, six of them starts, in 2018. He's been a full-time starter since then, with uneven results, though he had his most effective season in 2021, the redo of his junior season. Kafka has all the ingredients to have the chance to start, beginning with his 6-foot-4 frame and continuing with what could be a solid three-pitch mix. He relies heavily on his sinker-slider combination, with a fastball that sits in the low 90s but touches 95 mph, featuring a ton of sink and bore to it that generates a lot of ground-ball outs. His low-80s slider is a solidly average pitch that is his best strikeout pitch and while he has feel for his changeup, he didn't go to it much this year as he leaned on the slider more. The right-hander did miss more bats this spring than he had previously, though he still struggled with his command at times, with some scouts wondering why he gets hit as much as he does given how good his stuff can be. He should get the opportunity to begin pro ball as a starter, knowing that his sinker-slider combination could tick up in a big league bullpen if needed.

An unheralded transfer who went undrafted out of high school and during two years at Palomar (Calif.) JC, Olthoff recorded a 0.32 ERA in four starts at Tulane during the shortened 2020 season, ranking third in NCAA Division I in strikeouts (47, including 16 in one game against Cal State Fullerton) and sixth in WHIP (0.54). He hasn't been as spectacular this spring and missed an April start with a sore forearm, but he still has been one of the best strike-throwers in college baseball and finished fifth in D-I in K/BB ratio (91/11). The Green Wave's best pitching prospect since 2008 supplemental first-rounder Shooter Hunt, he should get picked around the fifth round. Olthoff's low-80s changeup is his lone plus offering, one he sells with fastball arm speed before it dives and disappears at the plate. His heater has pedestrian velocity at 88-91 mph and a peak of 93, but it plays up thanks to his sink and command. His upper-70s slider can be a solid pitch at times and he can manipulate it into a curveball with similar velocity but more depth. Olthoff has tremendous feel for pitching, and his ability to tunnel and mix his pitches gives him a high floor as a back-of-the-rotation starter. He has an effortless, upright delivery with a short stride that adds deception, and he can both pound the strike zone or get hitters to chase his secondary pitches off the plate. He doesn't have much margin for error but rarely makes mistakes, and he was 180 innings and three-plus seasons into his college career before he surrendered his first homer in late April.

A Texas state high school swimming champion in the 50-meter freestyle, David began his college career at McLennan (Texas) CC but pitched just 18 1/3 innings as a freshman and redshirted in 2019 because he struggled to throw strikes. His velocity has picked up since he transferred to Northwestern State and while he still battles the strike zone, he has one of the best curveballs in the 2021 Draft. He had a 72 percent swing-and-miss rate on his breaking ball and hitters went just 5-for-98 with 80 strikeouts against it, the main reason he ranked third in NCAA Division I in whiff rate (15.3 per nine innings). David's curveball can be a plus-plus hammer at its best, sitting at 83-85 mph and reaching 87 with tremendous depth, and he has doubled its usage to nearly 40 percent this spring. When he doesn't stay on top of his breaker, it plays like a mid-80s slider that's just as unhittable. He also can run his fastball up to 99 mph and uses his 6-foot-5 frame to create downhill plane to compensate for a lack of life, though he often dials it back to 92-95 in order to try to throw strikes. David barely uses a changeup and has averaged nearly a walk per inning in college, so he almost certainly will be a reliever in the long run. He pitches without a windup but still struggles to repeat his delivery, which features some effort. He's strong and athletic and scouts praise his competitiveness and intelligence, which helped him graduate in May with a business administration degree and a 4.0 grade-point average.

Jordan starred for two years at Cowley County (Kan.) CC and was drafted in the 31st round by the Rays in 2019. While he can flash a mid-90s fastball and a plus slurve at times, his velocity was down to 88-92 mph for much of this season and he had problems finding the strike zone. He barely uses his changeup and projects more as a reliever than a starter in pro ball.

Baker led Angelo State to the semifinals of the NCAA Division II College World Series, setting school records for single-season (13) and career (32) victories and career strikeouts (298 in 285 innings). He has a strong 6-foot-3 frame and can reach 96 mph with his fastball, though it straightens out at times. His slider ranges from fringy to solid and he also employs a decent changeup.

Dion won Southland Conference Pitcher of the Year after going 9-4 with a 3.07 ERA and a school-record 121 strikeouts in 99 2/3 innings. He commands his 87-91 mph fastball and pair of breaking pitches well while also earning high marks for his competitiveness.

Winkel was a solid high school prospect in Connecticut, one whom the Yankees drafted late in 2018, but decided to honor his commitment to his home-state UConn. A strong freshman season in 2019 got people excited, but he missed 2020 following Tommy John surgery. He came back healthy in 2021, but while he threw better than expected, his offensive performance was a bit more uneven. Winkel does fit a profile teams covet as a left-handed hitting catcher with power. He has above-average raw pop and did get to it in 2021 to hit 11 homers, but while he made a lot of contact and drew walks overall, he did most of his damage against fastballs, struggling against breaking stuff and leading some to wonder if there's enough hit tool for him to keep getting to that power consistently at the next level. While he's not a runner, Winkel is athletic behind the plate and is an average overall receiver and his arm bounced back better than anticipated, giving scouts confidence he'll be just fine behind the plate. That, plus the raw power are enough to think he might be a backup as a pro, with the chance for him to be more if the bat bounces back overall.

An infielder at the start of his college career, Schultz became a full-time outfielder in 2021 and hit .329/.401/.481 with 22 steals. A plus runner, he has some pop but gets into trouble when he hunts power and his right-handed swing gets too long. With his quickness and average arm, his best defensive options are center field and second base.

Litwicki has earned some Trevor Rosenthal comparisons this spring after switching from a two-seam fastball to a four-seamer that averages 96 mph and peaks at 99. His low-80s slider has its moments too, but he pitched just 12 innings in 2021 while dealing with back and shoulder issues. Strictly a reliever, he's in his fourth year at Indiana after missing 2018 following Tommy John surgery.

A top-five-rounds talent as a Tennessee high schooler, Fisher went undrafted in 2017 because of his Vanderbilt commitment. He pitched just 55 innings in four years with the Commodores, in part because he missed 2020 following Tommy John surgery and never consistently threw strikes. But he's still a southpaw who can provide mid-90s fastballs and low-80s sliders out of the bullpen, and his low three-quarters arm slot and some crossfire in his delivery make him tough on left-handers. If he can refine his control, he could develop into a late-inning bullpen option.

Three years ago, Troy had a twitchy 6-foot-1, 185-pound center fielder who went in the fifth round of the Draft, and Brandon Lockridge since has become one of the Yankees' better position prospects. Now the Trojans have another center fielder with the same build and profile who figures to go in the same range this July. Part of two national championship teams at Parkview High (Lilburn, Ga.), Cerny offers one of the best combinations of raw power and speed in the 2021 crop. The question with Cerny is how much he'll hit, because he has a busy right-handed swing and a grip-it-and-rip-it approach. He chases a lot of pitches out of the strike zone and struggles with breaking balls and changeups. But his bat speed and strength produce legitimate pop from gap to gap when he connects, and he could have 25-homer power if he can make more consistent contact. Cerny's plus-plus speed and aggressive nature on the basepaths make him a dynamic basestealing threat. His quickness allows him to cover plenty of ground and combines with his defensive instincts to make him a quality center fielder.
